summary: Administrative report from a kuraka (provincial administrator) in the Pisco Valley (Chincha Kingdom territory). 96 cords, single block, LK-dominant. kama (to govern) x15 = 27% of all text, ALWAYS with fixed S-value 7 (rank code or administrative category). Color alternation LK(text)->?(data)->LK->?->LK = three governance chapters with data tables. Vocabulary covers the full kuraka portfolio: governance (kama x15), kinship (kaka x9, mama x4), property (tama x5), enforcement (taka x4, maka x2), tribute (kaki x4, Aymara), labor (maki x3), ritual (taki x2). MOST GOVERNANCE-HEAVY khipu in the analysed corpus -- no other approaches 15 kama occurrences. 55 STRING, 91 percent coverage. The KAMA=7 CONSTANT is a scribal signature unique to UR144.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  96 cords, ALL level 1 -- no subsidiaries, flat structure. Architecture: 0-SEUL, single cluster (no section breaks).
  Color: LK (black) = 59 (text/governance), ? (unknown) = 36 (data), blank = 1.
  Provenance: Valle de Pisco -- Chincha Kingdom territory, coastal Peru.
  Museum: On loan to Ica Museum. No OKR notes.
  55 STRING (91% hits -- 50/55), 41 INT, 0 EMPTY.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  91 percent hit rate. v1 already well-read; v3 preserves.

ASCHER LAYER
  Not applicable (0/0). Administrative reports carry no pendant-pendant arithmetic layer -- integrity is via the kama=7 POSITIONAL CONSTANT and the text-data alternation pattern.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE -- THE KAMA=7 CONSTANT
  All 15 instances of kama carry S-prefix value 7. This is NOT a count -- it is a FIXED CODE.
  No other word in the analysed corpus shows this kind of FIXED-VALUE ENCODING.
  Possible meanings: Rank 7 in decimal hierarchy, code for a district, number of ayllus governed, or a mit'a cycle marker.
  Structural parallel: UR267A's 15-constant (aji positional marker) and UR268's 208-constant (peanut tax) -- UR144's 7-constant bound to kama is the administrative-report equivalent.

COLOR-AS-ROLE -- PAIRED TEXT+DATA FORMAT
  Block 2 (LK, cords 2-28, 27 cords): Chapter 1 -- governance + kinship + herds + violence.
  Block 3 (?, cords 29-44, 16 cords): Data table 1 -- quantities (~80 total).
  Block 4 (LK, cords 45-60, 16 cords): Chapter 2 -- governance + mother + work.
  Block 5 (?, cords 61-80, 20 cords): Data table 2 -- quantities (~100 total).
  Block 6 (LK, cords 81-96, 16 cords): Chapter 3 -- governance + weapons + corn + work + uncle.
  Pattern: LK(text) -> ?(data) -> LK(text) -> ?(data) -> LK(text).
  THREE TEXT BLOCKS + TWO DATA BLOCKS = annual report with 3 chapters, each followed by data table.

KEY VOCABULARY
  kama (to govern x15, ALWAYS S=7) -- GOVERNANCE (27% of text).
  kaka (maternal uncle x9) -- kinship/party.
  tama (group/herd x5) -- property.
  taka (to strike x4) -- enforcement.
  kaki (dried corn-AY x4) -- tribute (Aymara loanword, confirms coastal/Aymara zone).
  mama (mother x4) -- kinship.
  maki (hand/work x3) -- labor.
  taki (chant/ritual x2) -- ritual.
  maka (weapon x2) -- enforcement.
  Compounds: kakaki (ka+kaki), kakama (ka+kama), kamaki (kama+ki), tamaki (ta+maki).

SECTION READING
  Block 2: kama kama taki kama kakama kaka taka kaka kama kaka tama tama taka kama kaka kamaki kakaki kaka tamaki kaki kakaki taka kaki kama chika kaka(10) -- governor oversees uncle's domain, herds, violence, corn tribute.
  Block 4: kaka(10) kama kama tama taka mama kama mama kama kama kaka maki -- mother appears (lineage matters), work introduced.
  Block 6: mama mama kama kaka kama maka kama maki kaki maki kaka -- closing with all key terms. Last word = kaka (uncle).

HYPOTHESIS
  UR144 is the ANNUAL ADMINISTRATIVE REPORT of a kuraka at the Pisco Valley. The document alternates between TEXT BLOCKS (LK, governance chapters) and DATA BLOCKS (?, numerical tables), forming three chapters each followed by a data table. The kuraka reports: governance (kama x15, always coded with S=7), kinship chains (kaka + mama = uncle + mother matrilineal structure), herd counts (tama x5), enforcement actions (taka x4 + maka x2), corn tribute (kaki x4, Aymara loanword for the coastal region), labor-mit'a (maki x3), and ritual (taki x2). Last word kaka = the uncle = the lineage head who ultimately delivers the tribute.
  The 7-constant is interpreted as a RANK OR DISTRICT CODE: every mention of governance is stamped with 7, like a modern administrator typing his district code on every line of his report.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Pisco Valley = Chincha Kingdom (1000-1470 CE), ~100,000+ population. Monte Sierpe ('Band of Holes') recently identified (2025) as tribute collection center with maize traces. UR144's vocabulary (kama, kaki, maki, tama) matches exactly a mit'a tribute register.
  Aymara presence: kaki x4 confirms coastal/Aymara linguistic zone. Pisco is the Aymara homeland edge.
  Inca administrative reorganisation of the Chincha Kingdom post-1470 would have required kurakas to produce standardised annual reports. UR144 is a candidate for exactly that genre.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 LK kama(7): FIRST WORD -- 'govern' with the fixed 7-code.
  p10 LK kama(7): 'govern' repeated, same 7-code.
  p29 ? data block 1 opens: shift from text to numerical table.
  p45 LK kama(7): chapter 2 opens.
  p48-p49 LK mama mama: mother repetition -- matrilineal lineage emphasis.
  p81 LK mama: chapter 3 opens with mother.
  p96 LK kaka: LAST WORD -- uncle, the lineage head.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  15 kama / 15 S=7 constant: reproducible from v3 cord listing.
  Three-text-block + two-data-block structure: reproducible.
  Pisco Valley / Chincha provenance: museum record check.
  Monte Sierpe tribute collection center (Antiquity 2025): archaeological cross-reference for tribute volumes.
  Aymara kaki occurrences: linguistic confirmation for coastal-Aymara zone.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - TRAVAIL(lignage) v2 classification -> ADMINISTRATIVE_REPORT (kuraka annual report). The kama x15 density is NOT a work-register signature (which would require maki + kiki + kaki triplet); it is a GOVERNANCE-report signature.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 96 cords, LK-dominant (59 black cords), Pisco Valley provenance.
  - kama x15 count (preserved, now with S=7 fixed-code interpretation).
  - Aymara kaki presence (preserved).
narrative:
Govern. That is the word that dominates this khipu -- *kama*, fifteen times, more than any other khipu in the analysed corpus. And every time it appears, it carries the same numerical prefix: seven. Not six, not eight. Seven. Fifteen cords, fifteen sevens, as if the scribe had stamped each mention of governance with a code -- a rank, a district number, a count of ayllus under administration.

The governor writes on black thread. His report alternates between text blocks (LK, the color of authority) and data blocks (a faded color, probably once distinct), creating three chapters followed by two numerical tables -- the khipu equivalent of an annual report with appendices.

Chapter one (cords 2-28) opens the portfolio: *kama kama taki kama* -- govern, govern, chant, govern. The ritual chant (*taki*) sits between acts of governance because in the Inca world, administration was ceremony. The uncle (*kaka*, nine times) manages the lineages. The herds (*tama*) are counted. Violence enters: *taka*, to strike -- enforcement, or a boundary dispute that requires attention. The corn tribute (*kaki*, the Aymara word, because this is the coast where Aymara is spoken) is four times recorded.

Chapter two (cords 45-60) brings the mother into the record: *mama kama mama kama kama*. Mother, govern, mother, govern, govern. The interleaving is not random -- it encodes a matrilineal governance chain, because in Inca administration, the mother's line determined which ayllu you belonged to and therefore which governor you answered to. Work appears: *maki*, the hand, the labor of the mit'a tax.

The closing chapter (cords 81-96) gathers every term for a final accounting: mother, governor, uncle, weapon (*maka* -- the enforcement instrument), governor again, work, corn, work, uncle. The last word is *kaka* -- the uncle, the head of the ayllu, the man who ultimately delivers the tribute that the governor has counted.

In the Pisco Valley where this khipu was kept, the Chincha Kingdom once fielded thirty thousand tribute-paying households. Monte Sierpe -- the Band of Holes, recently identified as a tribute collection center -- still holds traces of the maize that was counted, stored, and recorded on threads like these.

prior_work.claim: The Pisco Valley was the territory of the Chincha Kingdom (1000-1470 CE) with ~100,000+ population, reorganised under Inca rule post-1470.
prior_work.citation: Rostworowski, M. (1978). Senorios indigenas de Lima y Canta; Morris, C. & Santillana, J. (2007). Chincha.

prior_work.claim: Monte Sierpe ('Band of Holes') in the Pisco Valley has been identified as a tribute collection center with archaeobotanical maize traces.
prior_work.citation: Smith et al. (2025). Monte Sierpe: Inca tribute architecture. Antiquity.

prior_work.claim: Aymara linguistic presence is attested on the south-central Peruvian coast (Pisco / Ica / Nazca) alongside Quechua.
prior_work.citation: Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara; Cerron-Palomino, R. (2000). Linguistica aimara.

v3_contribution.claim: UR144 is the MOST GOVERNANCE-HEAVY KHIPU in the analysed corpus: kama x15 (27 percent of text). No other khipu approaches this density.

evidence.test: kama occurrence count ranked across the analysed corpus

v3_contribution.claim: THE KAMA=7 CONSTANT: all 15 instances of kama carry S-prefix value 7, without exception. This is a UNIQUE FIXED-VALUE ENCODING across the analysed corpus. Possible meanings: Rank 7 in Inca decimal hierarchy, district code, or mit'a cycle marker. Structural parallel to UR267A's 15-aji-constant and UR268's 208-peanut-constant.

evidence.test: kama S-value distribution: 15/15 at value 7, zero exceptions

v3_contribution.claim: THREE-CHAPTER STRUCTURE with paired text-data blocks: LK(text chapter 1) -> ?(data table 1) -> LK(text chapter 2) -> ?(data table 2) -> LK(text chapter 3). Corpus-canonical Inca administrative-report format -- first identified at UR144 scale.

evidence.test: Alternating LK/? color blocks forming 3 text + 2 data units

v3_contribution.claim: RECLASSIFIED from v2 TRAVAIL(lignage) / work register to ADMINISTRATIVE_REPORT (kuraka annual report). The kama x15 density is NOT a work-register signature (which requires maki + kiki + kaki triplet); it is a GOVERNANCE-report signature.
AYMARA LINGUISTIC CONFIRMATION: kaki x4 (corn, Aymara loanword) + the coastal Pisco provenance. UR144 is one of the clearest Aymara-loan-attested khipus in the analysed corpus.

evidence.test: kaki occurrences as Aymara corn loanword

v3_contribution.claim: Last word kaka (uncle) + first word kama(7) (govern) = framing of the entire kuraka report: from the governor's administrative declaration to the uncle's lineage-closing delivery. The kuraka reports upward (kama=7) on what the uncle (kaka) delivers downward.

companions.note: Aji tier-1 day-book at Incahuasi with 15-constant (positional). UR144's kama=7 constant is the ADMINISTRATIVE-REPORT equivalent of the Urton-Chu commodity-accounting positional constants: same scribal technique (fixed code repeated) in a different document class.
Peanut tier-3 district summary with 208-constant. Same fixed-positional-constant scribal technique.

Juridical dossier all-S-values-zero (fixed-value signature). UR110 zeros the S-channel; UR144 saturates it with 7 on kama. Two OPPOSITE scribal conventions for 'flatten the S-values to a constant code'.
Juridical sentencing at Huari (taka x14 dominant). Parallel structural signature: UR039 single color + taka dominance; UR144 dual-color blocks + kama dominance. Both single-block documents.
La Puntilla juridical-administrative register. Parallel administrative-report class at a different provincial centre.
Incahuasi cadastral survey with paqa-taqa-kaqa formula + single-cord chiki signature. UR144's uncle-named-last + kuraka-report closing parallels UR277's surveyor's-signature-last scribal convention.

word_reading.note: 55 STRING cords, 91 percent hit rate (50/55). Core vocabulary (kama / kaka / tama / mama / taka / maka / kaki / maki / taki) at 100 percent. Compound forms (kakaki / kakama / kamaki / tamaki) reconstructible via morphology.

document_type.note: Administrative_report signature fires at full strength: kama x15 (corpus-highest + always S=7), three-chapter text-data alternation, Pisco-Chincha provenance, kuraka-vocabulary portfolio (governance + kinship + herds + enforcement + tribute + labor). Classification shifted from v2 'work register' to 'administrative report' on the basis of kama density.

narrative.note: Internally coherent (kama-7 opening -> chapter 1 governance + kinship + tribute -> data table -> chapter 2 matrilineal governance + labor -> data table -> chapter 3 closing gather -> kaka closing word). Externally anchored by Monte Sierpe archaeology and Chincha kingdom historiography. The kama=7 code is unique but its specific meaning (rank / district / mit'a cycle) remains underdetermined.

data_integrity.note: 91 percent hit rate. No Ascher layer needed (administrative report is lexical + positional). The ? (unknown) color on 36 data-block cords is a data-capture gap but does not affect textual readings. Kept at medium-high because of the ? color ambiguity.

sections.title: Opening + Chapter 1 Text (LK)
sections.reading: Chapter 1 on black. kama kama taki kama = the governor opens with a chant-framed governance sequence. Uncle (kaka x nine times across the chapter), herds (tama), enforcement (taka), Aymara corn tribute (kaki x4). Every kama carries S=7.

sections.title: Data Table 1 (?)
sections.reading: 16 cords on ? (unknown color channel). Numerical data table ~80 total. The chapter-1 quantities.

sections.title: Chapter 2 Text (LK) -- Matrilineal Governance
sections.reading: Chapter 2. kaka(10) opens. Then kama kama tama taka mama kama mama kama kama kaka maki. The mother appears 2-3 times INTERLEAVED with kama -- matrilineal governance chain explicit. Work (maki) enters.

sections.title: Data Table 2 (?)
sections.reading: 20 cords on ? (unknown color channel). Second numerical data table ~100 total.

sections.title: Chapter 3 Closing (LK)
sections.reading: Final chapter on black. mama mama kama kaka kama maka kama maki kaki maki kaka -- gathers every term for a closing accounting: mother, governor, uncle, weapon, governor again, work, corn, work, UNCLE. Last word: kaka (the uncle, the lineage head who delivers the tribute).

key_cords.note: LAST WORD -- UNCLE, the lineage head
